topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany door clicked shut, the sound echoing in the sterile waiting room. Liam clutched the strap of his worn backpack so tightly his knuckles were white. He hadn't expected the consultation to be with Marcus Thorne, the boy who used to dismantle his sense of self in the schoolyard. Marcus, now impeccably dressed in a tailored suit, sat behind a desk that looked more like an executive office than a therapy space. Liam swallowed, the dryness in his throat making the action difficult. Marcus looked up, his expression calm, almost serene, a stark contrast to the sneer Liam remembered from seventh grade. "So," Marcus began, his voice low and measured, "tell me about the pressure you felt regarding your academic performance." Liam shifted his weight, his gaze darting everywhere but directly at Marcus’s eyes. He mumbled something incoherent, his breath catching in his chest. Every muscle in his jaw felt tight, a physical knot refusing to loosen. He wanted to bolt, to run back to the familiar sting of confrontation, but he remained rooted to the spot, paralyzed by the unfamiliar gravity of the situation.

[story 2]

I kept glancing at the framed photograph on the credenza—a blurry snapshot of us, years ago, Marcus grinning with a smirk that used to feel like a physical blow. Now, he was Dr. Thorne, radiating an aura of quiet authority that made my skin prickle. When he leaned forward, resting his elbows on the desk, the scent of expensive cologne replaced the stale scent of cheap deodorant I associated with him. "It takes courage to seek help, doesn't it?" he asked, his tone gentle, almost patronizing. My hands began to tremble, a faint, uncontrolled vibration that I desperately tried to suppress by pressing my fingers against my thighs. I could feel a flush creeping up my neck, hot and undeniable. Every word he spoke felt like a probe, searching for the vulnerabilities I had spent years burying under layers of defensiveness. I managed a weak nod, my throat suddenly constricted, unable to form a coherent response.

[story 3]

The silence stretched between us, thick and heavy, punctuated only by the faint hum of the air conditioning. Marcus was articulating complex psychological concepts with an ease that was both mesmerizing and deeply unsettling. He spoke about trauma and coping mechanisms as if they were mundane weather patterns. I found myself physically recoiling, leaning back in the uncomfortable leather chair until the edge dug into my spine. My breathing became shallow, rapid, as if I were trying to suck in too much air. When he paused, waiting for my input, the anticipation was agonizing. I opened my mouth, intending to voice some defense, some sharp retort, but nothing came out. My eyes flickered towards the door, searching for an escape route that didn't exist. The sheer unfamiliarity of being subjected to such careful, insightful scrutiny left me feeling exposed, stripped bare under the bright, unforgiving light of his professional gaze.
